coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of ternary carbon dots (CDs)/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocomposite via MOF templating as a reusable heterogeneous nanocatalyst and nano-photocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Gholizadeh Khasevani, S.
Shahsavari, S.
Gholami, M.R.
coredata.dc:description: An environmentally friendly heterogeneous ternary carbon dots (CDs)/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocomposite was synthesized by a multistep method. In this study, we have studied low-temperature calcination with relatively inexpensive reactants. The sufficient CD arrays were generated by loading the glucose molecules into the pores of an as-obtained metal-organic framework (MOF), i.e. MIL-88B (Fe)), by heating to 200 °C. Fabricated nanocomposite structures were characterized by FT-IR, XRD, EIS, SEM, TEM, BET, and TGA techniques, demonstrating the decoration of the Bi2S3 on the CDs/MIL-88B (Fe) nanocomposite surface. MOF templating has been chosen as a systemic method for limited size CD formation inside MIL-88B (Fe) pores. The CD arrays also revealed an excellent efficacy for catalytic and photocatalytic applications. According to the experimental results, CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 with the highest electron-accepting and transporting properties capacity displayed the most increased activity as catalyst and photocatalyst. The catalytic and photocatalytic performance of obtained structures was investigated by reducing 4-nitrophenol (4-Nip) to 4-aminophenol (4-Amp) and decomposition of cationic Methylene Blue (MB) and anionic Acid Blue 92 (AB92) dyes under visible light irradiation, respectively. The current work exhibited CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocatalyst as a potent multifunctional composite to remove organic components in the presence of reducing agents and visible light, which can be employed for wastewater treatment applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reduction reaction
Photodegradation
Carbon dot arrays
Metal-organic framework

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of Catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol and organic dyes photodegradation
Structural and morphological characteristics of as-prepared samples
Evaluation of the catalytic activity of nanocatalysts
A possible mechanism for catalytic performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction patterns of as-prepared materials.
SEM images of Bi2S3 (a, b), CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 (c, d), and EDS images of CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3, (e, f).
TEM image of Bi2S3 a) and CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 b).
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms: (a) Bi2S3 and (b) CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3, BJH pore size distribution plots inset.
UV–vis absorption spectra of reduction of 4‐Nip by NaBH4 over structures.
(a) Plots of Ct/C0 versus re of as-prepared catalysts ((4‐Nip) = 0.1 mM and (NaBH4) = 0.1 M). (b) Plots of ln(Ct/C0) of 4‐Nip versus reaction time for the as-prepared catalysts.
The photocatalytic results for decomposition of a) MB and c) AB92 and removal over nanocomposites. b) and d) Linear-log plot under visible radiation.
Recycled photoactivity of a) MB, b) AB92 over CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocomposite under visible light irradiation.
Effect of various trapping species on the decomposition of a) MB, b) AB92 over CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocomposite.
The suggested mechanism for reduction of 4‐Nip by NaBH4 over CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 structure.
Possible photodecomposition mechanism for CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocomposite under LED light.
Results of specific surface areas (SBET), pore volumes (Pvol), and pore diameters (Pdiam) evaluation of Bi2S3and CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of ternary carbon dots (CDs)/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocomposite via MOF templating as a reusable heterogeneous nanocatalyst and nano-photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Azadi Avenue, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Azadi Avenue, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An environmentally friendly heterogeneous ternary carbon dots (CDs)/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocomposite was synthesized by a multistep method. In this study, we have studied low-temperature calcination with relatively inexpensive reactants. The sufficient CD arrays were generated by loading the glucose molecules into the pores of an as-obtained metal-organic framework (MOF), i.e. MIL-88B (Fe)), by heating to 200 °C. Fabricated nanocomposite structures were characterized by FT-IR, XRD, EIS, SEM, TEM, BET, and TGA techniques, demonstrating the decoration of the Bi2S3 on the CDs/MIL-88B (Fe) nanocomposite surface. MOF templating has been chosen as a systemic method for limited size CD formation inside MIL-88B (Fe) pores. The CD arrays also revealed an excellent efficacy for catalytic and photocatalytic applications. According to the experimental results, CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 with the highest electron-accepting and transporting properties capacity displayed the most increased activity as catalyst and photocatalyst. The catalytic and photocatalytic performance of obtained structures was investigated by reducing 4-nitrophenol (4-Nip) to 4-aminophenol (4-Amp) and decomposition of cationic Methylene Blue (MB) and anionic Acid Blue 92 (AB92) dyes under visible light irradiation, respectively. The current work exhibited CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocatalyst as a potent multifunctional composite to remove organic components in the presence of reducing agents and visible light, which can be employed for wastewater treatment applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the increasing development of petrochemical, pharmaceutical, and industrial activities has produced an enormous amount of undesirable byproducts that cannot be released through effluents to the environment. Therefore, water treatment is in high demand for eliminating such hazardous organic pollutants, which can contaminate the environment and threaten the health of humans and other living organisms [1–4]. In this regard, several strategies have been proposed, including adsorption, biological treatment, and (photo)catalytic reduction [5–8]. Efficient, reversible, and affordable (photo)catalysts can be employed as a remarkable solution for this concern [9,10]. Photocatalytic degradation based on semiconductor catalysts under visible light irradiation of the sun can be introduced as a promising and green method for dye degradation [11–13]. Semiconductor nanostructures are able to produce electron-hole pairs to react with organic dyes through reduction and oxidation reactions and deliver water and CO2 as eco-friendly products [12,14,15]. An applied semiconductor photocatalyst should have a bandgap with an energy level within one’s range for visible photons. In the last decade, research studies have shifted to the development of visible light photocatalyst instead of traditional photocatalysts such as ZnO and TiO2, which have ultraviolet (UV) radiation range bandgap, due to the sparse amount of UV radiation in the solar spectrum [15–17]. Among semiconductors, p-type Bi2S3 with a low bandgap around 1.3 eV and high-performance absorption is a proper candidate for photocatalytic reactions [18,19]. In order to increase the amounts of electron-hole pairs, enhancing separation time and preventing the recombination of the electron-hole pairs, Bi2S3 should be decorated on a catalytic base and binds to other metallic, carbonic, and another semiconductor (p-n junction) nanostructures to be optimized for high-quality photocatalytic activities [20–22].

Decoration of (photo)catalysts on hollow and porous structures can facilitate stability, photoinduced charge transfer, and even increase absorbed photons [23,24]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a well-known family of porous structures made of metal ions and organic linkers [25–27]. MOFs have been utilized for gas storage, dye adsorption, drug delivery, and notable for catalytic studies [28,29]. MIL-88B (Fe), as an iron-based MOF has advantages such as high porosity, high availability of metal sources, high light absorption, and efficient participation in photocatalytic reactions, which nominate it as an important choice for a photocatalyst nanocomposite formation [30,31].

Carbon dots (CDs) are referred to a collection of carbon materials with at least one dimension less than 10 nanometers [32,33]. These carbon nanostructures have high visible light absorption capacity, high charge transfer capability, and most importantly, the hindering effect on electron-hole pairs recombination phenomena [34,35]. These materials can be easily synthesized in the presence of a carbon source using thermal methods and have shown low toxicity and biocompatibility [36,37]. In this work, we used a novel method for CD synthesis based on the MOF templating. According to this method, the MOF pores act as a template to generate CDs with distinct sizes. Glucose (C6H12O6) is a rich source of carbon and can be carbonized at low temperature (<200 °C). The glucose with small size can be easily loaded into the pores of the MOF [38]. In this work, MOF (MIL-88B (Fe)) has been used as the host template with pore diameter of 30.27 nm.

Herein, we designed to decorate - CDs and Bi2S3 nanoparticles on the MIL-88B (Fe) MOF through thermal and hydrothermal reactions. The novel band-engineered heterogeneous nanocatalyst has a reversible and efficient photocatalytic effect on the degradation of acid blue 92 dye and an extraordinary catalytic reaction efficiency and rate to reduce 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol. The (photo)catalytic kinetics and mechanism of reactions were studied in this work as well.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis was performed on a Philips X’pert instrument employing Cu-Kα radiation (λ =0.1542 nm) at 40 kV/ 40 mA. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) data were recorded using a PerkinElmer Spectrum with a component pressed in KBr. FESEM images were recorded using a field emission scanning electron microscopy (TESCAN MIRA-3) instrument. Transmission Electron microscope (TEM) micrographs were obtained with a a Zeiss EM 900 instrument. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) techniques were employed for study of geometrical characteristics such as determination of specific surface area and pore size of as prepared samples via N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms with surface area and pore size analyzer (BELSORPmini II). Electrochemical measurement was carried out by the Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat (EG&G model 263A, USA). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a Pyris Diamond TG-DTA instrument. The Ultraviolet and visible absorption spectrums were obtained by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (GBC Cintra 40).

The solvothermal method was utilized for the synthesis of the MIL-88B (Fe) structure. In this process, 50 mL of DMF solvent has added to a beaker occupied with 1.35 g of FeCl3.6H2O and stirred for 20 min. Afterwards, 0.83 g of Terephthalic acid (H2BDC) was dissolved in 50 mL of DMF. After that, under stirring conditions, the solution of FeCl3.6H2O was added to the H2BDC solution. After 20 min of sonication process, the solution was poured into a Teflon autoclave maintained to 110 °C for 24 h. The prepared product was washed several times with DI water and dried overnight [27].

1.2 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 30 mL of ethylene glycol (EG) in a beaker under vigorous stirring, then 0.71 g of Thioacetamide (TAA) was added drop by drop into the solution with continuous stirring for 60 min. The mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and kept at 160 °C for 18 h. The resulting homogeneous mixture was centrifuged and washed with DI water and ethanol repeatedly and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 8 h [19].

A sufficient amount of MOF was dispersed into G solution (1 mM, EtOH/H2O: 9:1) for 7 h under stirring. After that, the sample was washed with EtOH/H2O (9:1) several times. G encapsulated MOF can be achieved after drying by nitrogen [38].

0.2 g of G/MIL-88B (Fe) was transferred to a tubular furnace at 200 °C under N2 flow for 2 h. G turned to CDs by this mild calcination method. After reaching room temperature, CDs/MIL-88B (Fe) nanocomposite can be collected, subsequently [38].

0.1 g of CDs/MIL-88B (Fe) was dispersed in 20 mL ethylene glycol under vigorous stirring for 1 h. Then Bi(NO3)3·5H2O solution and Thioacetamide (TAA) solution were added dropwisely into the mentioned solution and stirred for 1 h. Then, the resulting material was transferred to Teflon autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 18 h. The resulting mixture was washed with DI water and dry it on an oven overnight. Scheme 1 . Demonstrate the synthetic process of the as-prepared nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of Catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol and organic dyes photodegradation
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic process was measured through the reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol in the presence of NaBH4 aqueous. To obtain the reactant solution, 1 ml of NaBH4 aqueous solution (0.1 M) was poured into the 3 mL aqueous solution of 4-nitrophenol (0.1 mM). Consequently, 1 mg of the as-prepared sample was added to the reactant solution, and UV–vis absorption spectra were monitored till the peak the presence of the phenolate absorbance at 400 nm disappeared completely and generated the amino group peak at 300 nm. For the photocatalytic activity investigation of these structures in organic dyes degradation, 10 mg of various samples were introduced to the dye solution (100 mL) under stirring conditions. Before the irradiation, the solution was stirred for 2 h to reach the equilibrium adsorption of dyes on the nanocatalyst in the solution [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characteristics of as-prepared samples

ce:section.ce:para: XRD is used to analyze the purity and structural features of the as-obtained materials. Fig. 1 reveals all the diffraction peaks of pure and composite structures. The XRD patterns displayed a group of prominent diffraction peaks confirming the crystalline nature of the prepared nanocomposite. The diffraction peaks at 9°, 18°, 20.5° corresponded to MIL-88B (Fe) crystal. The crystallinity of MIL-88B (Fe) was not affected by the temperature-induced conversion [38]. The XRD result of CDs/MIL-88B (Fe) confirmed that the lattice of MOF has no alteration in the glucose particles presence since no peaks were seen in XRD data. Interestingly, the pure MOF pores were occupied with glucose [38]. Pure Bi2S3 demonstrated many different peaks at 15.7°, 17.7°, 22.4°, 24.8°, 28.7°, 31.6°, 45.3°, 46.4°, and 52.6° [40]. In addition, the result indicated that the main diffraction peaks of MOF were seen in the composite CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 verifying the stability of the crystal phase after the Bi2S3 modification.

The morphology of all obtained samples was investigated by FESEM. Fig. 2 (a,b) indicates the pure Bi2S3 structure in different size particles. Bi2S3 displayed irregular microspheres with an average diameter of 5 μm. Many crystalline nanorods shaped Bi2S3 microspheres with diameters of around 80 nm [41]. Fig. 2(c,d) demonstrates the morphology of the hetero-microspheres structures. The CDs/MOF surface is uniformly covered with the Bi2S3 microspheres. The MOF has been utilized as a template to synthesize pore size controllable CD particles [38]. Spindle-shaped morphology of MIL-88B (Fe) with an average size of 5.5 μm in length and 3.2 μm in diameter plays the controllable template role in the fabrication of nanometer-sized CDs with an average size of 1.5 nm [26,38]. As observed from Fig. 2(e,f), the EDS mapping images demonstrated that the Bi2S3 structure is loaded homogeneously onto the CDs/MIL-88B (Fe) nanocomposite.

TEM image of Bi2S3 spheres shows different size of the structure (Fig. 3 a). We tried to obtain an obvious TEM image of the ternary nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 3b CDs can be found like darker regions inside the MIL-88B (Fe) and Bi2S3 spheres have been decorated on the surface of CDs/MIL-88B (Fe) binary. It seems MIL-88B (Fe) has deformed during low temperature calcination and it is not a complete octahedron and has a deformed spindle-shaped morphology.

Geometrical characteristics such as specific surface area (SBET), pore volume (Pvol), and average pore diameter (Pdiam) of Bi2S3 and CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 were analyzed through N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms by BET and BJH methods (Fig. 4 a, b). Table 1 .shows the summary of obtained results.

FT-IR was performed to identify the functional groups of the prepared samples. Fig. 5 displayed the FT-IR spectra of CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocomposite compared with the pure samples. FTIR spectrum of pure Bi2S3 in Fig. 5 showed a band at 435 cm−1, 573 cm−1, 788 cm−1, and 857 cm−1 is associated with the BiS bonding [42]. The band at 618 cm−1 assigned to stretching vibrations of sulphur displaying the formation of Bi2S3 [43] and the band at 552 cm−1 is assigned to the intrinsic lattice vibrations of Fe–O of MOF [27]. The prominent band in 1667 cm−1, 1556 cm−1, 1392 cm−1, 1020 cm−1, and 750 cm−1 can be attributed to the carboxylate group vibrations [26]. The broadband at 3440 cm−1 has corresponded to OH stretching vibrations of absorbed water [25,26]. The IR absorbance confirmed the extent of CDs in the nanocomposite. The band of CDs at 3456 cm−1 and 1722 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH and CO [37]. Furthermore, the absorbance peaks at 1031 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 assigned the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of COC, respectively [38].

Thermogravimetric data (TGA) was investigated for prepared samples to get some information about their thermal decomposition (Fig. 6 ). The TGA curve displayed that the MIL-88B (Fe) has thermal stability in 280 °C under N2 atmosphere conditions [44]. Heating the binary structures (glucose-loaded MOF) to 280 °C, the glucose structure decomposes at 195 °C and converts to an array of carbon dots [39]. The conversion of the glucose to carbon dots in the CDs/MIL-88B (Fe) is evidenced by changing the structures’ color [26]. Furthermore, water and DMF molecules removal in the materials under the heating caused the mass loss.

The EIS analysis was measured to study the discharge-charge process essential behavior in the electrode samples (Fig. 7 ). The impedance of synthesized samples was investigated at different frequencies between 0.1 Hz and 1 MHz at open circuit potential (OCP) in the aqueous solution containing 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]: K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) [31]. The EIS results indicated the Nyquist plots of the pure and nanocomposite electrodes. All of the structures showed a clear semicircle at higher frequencies and a straight line at lower frequency region, confirming that the electrode method recognized the charge carrier transfer impedance [30]. According to Fig. 7, the CDs /MIL-88B (Fe) can be effective connect with Bi2S3, and the primary reason for increasing electrical contact with the current collector verified the leading role of CDs/MIL-88B (Fe) in the charge/discharge reaction process and conducting to higher electrical mobility. To sum up, CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocomposite demonstrated a higher interfacial electron carrier; thus, photocatalytic activity and catalyst performance is remarkably increased [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the catalytic activity of nanocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The model reduction reaction of 4-Nip to 4-Amp was used in the presence of NaBH4 to investigate the catalytic activity of as-synthesized samples. By introducing NaBH4, the absorption peak of 4-Nip (320 nm) dramatically shifts to 400, which represents 4-nitrophenolate (4-NPh) ions formation. As soon as the addition of the catalyst, the 4-NPh absorption peak (400 nm) decreased and vanished in the absence of any light irradiation. Upon the progress of the reaction 4-Amp peak at 300 nm appeared and the yellow solution converted to a colorless one.

For kinetic analysis, 1 mg of prepared catalysts were added to the mentioned solution, and the absorption peak shifting (400 to 300 nm) and 4-Amp product formation was monitored by spectrophotometer. It is noteworthy that the addition of carbon dots affected the rate of reduction and enhanced catalytic enactment by assisting electron relay from BH4 − to 4-Nip [44,45]. The acquired results of the sample with deposition of carbon dots on MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 revealed the highest catalytic efficiency compared to Bi2S3, MIL-88B (Fe), and CDs/MIL-88B (Fe), which demonstrate the synergistic effect of ternary composite for catalytic reduction of 4-Nip.

It seems there is a fast electron transfer from Bi2S3 to carbon dots. The notable catalytic activity can be led by the synergistic effect between the binary of carbon dot/ Bi2S3 co-catalyst. Furthermore, in CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 catalyst, the MIL-88B (Fe) essentially synthesized as a carrier for carbon dots and Bi2S3. The reduction of 4-Nip to 4-Amp is accomplished within 240 s in the presence of CDs/MIL-88B (Fe), Bi2S3, CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 while MIL-88B (Fe) cannot achieve the reaction under the same reaction condition (Fig. 8 ).

In the following order CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3> Bi2S3> CDs/MIL-88B (Fe) > MIL-88B (Fe) the rate constants were confirmed MIL-88B (Fe) as a MOF can provide an appropriate surface for growth and deposition of nanostructures for the 4-Nip reduction in the presence of NaBH4 solution.

The ln (Ct/C0) versus t plot can be utilized to calculate the rate constant values, in which C0, Ct is the initial concentration of 4-Nip and concentration of 4-nitrophenolate ions, t min after the start of the reaction, respectively [46,47]. For the aim of first-order curve fitting, the data has been analyzed, and the values for apparent rate constant (kapp) were attained from the slopes are 0.011s−1, 0.007s−1, 0.003s−1, and 0.0004s-1 for the CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3, Bi2S3, CDs/MIL-88B (Fe), and MIL-88B (Fe), respectively (Fig. 9 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: A possible mechanism for catalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the negative charge between BH4 − and 4-NPh, there is an apparent kinetic barrier between them, which does not allow the reaction to have progress. Nevertheless, the catalyst can adsorb many negative agents, causing the catalyst to proceed through the electron relay mechanism systematically. Based on this mechanism, both the reducing agent and the substrate adsorb onto the nanocatalyst surface. BH4 − as the reducing agent through the oxidation process change to borate ions and then free electrons. The free electrons are able to participate in 4-Nip reduction. The ternary catalyst conducts the electron in the best way in comparison with other compounds [48]. The difference of rate constant shed light on the fact that significantly the catalytic performance has improved in CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 composite. This is attributed to the hydrogen species formation on the catalyst surface, facilitating interaction with adsorbed 4-Nip to produce 4-Amp. Moreover, the synergistic effect between CDs and Bi2S3 leads to relay electrons to 4-Nip and conduct electrons to it is a crucial factor. Additionally, BH4 − hydrolysis on the surface to produce H2 as a strong reducing agent not only have a contribution to reduction but also maintain the catalyst activity by eliminating 4-Amp from the surface [49]. The result, as expected, confirmed the undeniable role of NaBH4 as intermediate since the intensity of observed peaks were quiet weak in its absence and vice versa (Scheme 2 ).

Photocatalytic decomposition of two different types of organic dye AB92 (anionic) and MB (cationic) was studied under visible light irradiation in the presence of nanocatalysts. CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3, Bi2S3, CDs/MIL-88B (Fe), and MIL-88B (Fe) were employed for photodegradation of both kinds of anionic and cationic organic dyes. CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 had the best performance among all catalysts in photoactivation by an LED light. Moreover, anionic dyes, because of their high adsorption on the catalyst surface, were targeted to possess a higher decomposition rate than that of the cationic substrate. Due to the positive surface charge of as-synthesized catalysts, electrostatic interaction with the surfaces facilitate anionic dye adsorption, explaining these phenomena [50,51]. As displayed in Fig. 10 . ln Ct/C0 versus t followed a linear trend, which illustrates that the photocatalytic reaction was a first-order where C0 and Ct were dye concentrations in the initial point and at t min, respectively, and k is the rate constant [52]. For the durability study, the experiment was performed in the same reaction conditions during four photocatalytic cycles. The ternary composite CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 proved high durability and stability, which is a crucial privilege for a high-performance photocatalyst.

The decomposition of cationic and anionic dyes is determined by utilizing various samples’ photoactivity under visible light. The results showed that, in the absence of catalyst and light, the initial concentration of substrate does not show any change, and CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 composite acted as the best-photoactivated agent in the degradation of organic dyes. Fig. 10 represents the photodegradation of MB dye under LED light by CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3, Bi2S3, CDs/MIL-88B (Fe), and MIL-88B (Fe) which had 60 %, 58 %, 51 % and, 29 % degradation, respectively. Additionally, the final results for degradation of AB92 dye were 75 %, 65 %, 55 %, and 34 % for CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3, Bi2S3, CDs/MIL-88B (Fe), and MIL-88B (Fe), respectively. Therefore, cationic dye (MB) has been degraded slower than anionic dyes (AB92), which means AB92 adsorb faster than MB on the photocatalyst because of the positively charged surface. The EIS was applied to investigate the photoelectrochemical activity that leads to the photodegradation results verification. The data analysis confirmed that as-prepared composite promotes the rate of charge separation of photogenerated e−/h+ pairs to act a critical part in the photocatalytic performance, representing the high capable interfacial charge transporters between the electron donor and acceptor [26,30]. To determine photostability of CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3, the recycling practically was performed for four consecutive cycles under visible light. Clearly, the CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 for degradation of organic dyes demonstrated a slight decline after four-cycle run. Thus, it could be concluded that the CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 nanocomposite is sufficiently stable along the photocatalytic activity. (Fig. 11 ) [53,54].

Organic dyes were eliminated by CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 photoactivation through the formation of different radical species. To investigate the mechanism, the addition of different trapping agents to the photocatalytic reaction before light irradiation was necessary to monitor the reaction process for recognition of active agents. Appropriate trapping agents for active species such as 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), tert-butanol (t-BuOH), and KI were used to scavenge superoxide radicals (
O2 −), hydroxyl radicals (
OH), and holes (h+), respectively. The study demonstrated the dramatic inhibition of MB and AB92 decomposition in the presence of BQ and t-BuOH, which indicated formation
O2- and
OH have the most important part in the degradation mechanism (Fig. 12 ). While by KI addition, the photodegradation was not affected significantly. Based on former results, mechanism can be explained according to the following equations (Scheme 3 )[53,54].
(1) Photocatalyst + hυ → Photocatalyst (eCB − + hVB +)
(2) eCB − +
O2- + 2 H+ → H2O2
(3) H2O2 +
O2 − →
OHads + O2 + OH-
(5) hVB + + OHads − →
OHads (4). hVB + + H2O →
OHFree + H+
(6) H2O2 + eCB − →
OHads + OH−
(7) OH− + hVB +→
OHads
(8)
OHads +
O2 − + dye → degradation products

The ternary CDs/MIL-88B (Fe)/Bi2S3 composite was prepared by multistep methods to consist of solvothermal, thermal, and hydrothermal, respectively. The formation and stability of carbon dots and Bi2S3 nanoparticles on MIL-88B (Fe) were studied by EDS mapping imaging and XRD spectra. Binary of carbon dots and Bi2S3 made a synergic effect in which the nanocomposite displayed widespread activity both as a catalyst and photocatalyst. The catalytic activity study was carried out by a benchmark reaction, i.e. the reduction of 4-Nip to 4-Amp. Besides, the ternary catalyst emerged as a photocatalyst for the degradation of both cationic (MB) and anionic (AB92) dyes under visible light irradiation. The electron realm capability of carbon dots and Bi2S3 was responsible for the catalytic reduction, while the enhanced electron-hole pair separation was responsible for the degradation of organic dyes in the presence of visible light. The kinetic data revealed that the conversion of 4-Nip to 4-Amp and MB/AB92 degradation obeyed the pseudo- first-order reaction and first-order reaction, respectively. In addition, the mechanism study revealed that OH and O
2 − radicals played significant roles as active species in the photodecomposition of organic model pollutants.

S. Gholizadeh Khasevani: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. S. Shahsavari: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. M.R. Gholami: Investigation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Dubey, N. Upmanyu, World, 6 (2017) 1-13.
M. Raeiszadeh, F. Taghipour, Water research, 164 (2019) 114959.
H. Salari, S.G. Khasevani, S.R. Setayesh, M.R. Gholami, Materials research innovations, 22 (2018) 137-143.
D. Ranjbar, M. Raeiszadeh, L. Lewis, M.J. MacLachlan, S.G. Hatzikiriakos, Cellulose, 27 (2020) 3211-3232.
